topics: 🪨 The chain-stone case: stumble on stone A, struck by stone B — who pays?
🍶 R' Yochanan: בור שלנזקין פטור על הכלים — pit-style damage exempts vessels
💧 Mishnah: broken jug, slipping in water or cut by shards
⚔️ R' Lazar vs. R' Yochanan — at the moment of falling vs. afterward
🌍 על מנת כן הנחיל יהושע לישראל את הארץ — Joshua's condition for the land

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Pits, Shards, and Joshua's Condition
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The daf opens with a sequence of stone-stumble cases that ask how far liability extends when a hazard is moved by wind or by a victim's own foot. R' Yochanan then states a foundational rule: בור שלנזקין פטור על הכלים — a בור-style hazard does not generate liability for damaged vessels, only for personal injury. The new Mishnah (3:2) adds the broken-jug case (slipping on water, cutting on shards) and R' Yehudah's distinction by intent. The Halakhah lays out R' Lazar / R' Yochanan's dispute about when exactly the dispute applies, and grounds the very right to leave damaged property in the road in the foundational principle that על מנת כן הנחיל יהושע לישראל את הארץ.
overview.hebrewSource: מִילְּתֵיהּ דְּרִבִּי יוֹחָנָן אָמְרָה. בּוֹר שֶׁלַּנִּזָּקִין פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים

slides.title: 🪨 Three Stumble-Stone Cases
slides.subtitle: The Yerushalmi probes how far the chain of causation can stretch.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Two stones in רה"ר. A pedestrian stumbles on the first and is struck by the second. Who pays — first owner or second?
2️⃣ A stone is placed in רה"ר. The wind blows it into a private domain; someone trips. Is the original owner liable?
3️⃣ A stone is placed in רה"ר. Someone kicks it (נתקל בה והתיזה) into a private domain where a third person trips. Liability of the original owner?
🔍 The Yerushalmi answers with the bull-pit baraita: when two causes converge, the rules of apportionment from Daf 12 carry over.
🎯 The deeper issue: every stumble-stone is a בור — the question is whether the original creator stays liable once nature or another person displaces it.

callout.label: Big picture:
callout.text: These three cases set up the next chiddush: even when liability does follow the stone, the pit-rules still cap it — vessels are not covered.

slides.title: 🍶 R' Yochanan: The Pit Pays for the Person, Not the Flask
slides.subtitle: A landmark formulation that will guide the rest of the chapter.

nodes.content:
הִנִּיחַ אַבְנוֹ וּמַשָּׂאוֹ בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים
Stone + load in the road. Pedestrian stumbles, holding a flask.

columns.label: Personal injury
columns.content:
חַיָיב עַל נִיזְקֵי אָדָם
Liable for damage to the person

columns.label: Flask (כלים)
columns.content:
פָּטוּר עַל נִיזְקֵי צְלוּחִיתוֹ
Exempt for damage to the flask

nodes.content:
בּוֹר שֶׁלַּנִּזָּקִין פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים
A בור hazard does not generate liability for vessel damage.

Exception: אִם כְּדֶרֶךְ הַטָּחָה הִטִּיחַ — אֲפִילוּ עַל נִיזְקֵי צְלוּחִית חַיָיב
If the impact was active 'walking' damage, even the flask is covered.

callout.label: Source of the rule:
callout.text: Torah's pit verse (וְנָפַל שָׁמָּה שּׁוֹר אוֹ חֲמוֹר) names animals — the gemara reads this as excluding כלים. R' Yochanan converts this exegetical move into a working halakhic principle.

slides.title: 💧 The Broken-Jug Mishnah (3:2)
slides.subtitle: Two ways to be hurt by your neighbor's broken vessel — and a Tannaitic split on intent.

boxes.title: 🟦 חכמים
items: Hold the owner liable in both modes
It is your hazard, your bill

boxes.title: 🟥 ר' יהודה
items: Reads ownership as ending when accident occurs

callout.label: Why intent matters:
callout.text: R' Yehudah holds that אָדָם מַבְקִיר נְזָקָיו בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים — a person is presumed to have abandoned (made hefker) his damaged property in the road. Without intent to harm, no liability.

slides.title: 💬 R' Lazar vs. R' Yochanan — When Is the Dispute?
slides.subtitle: Pinpointing the moment R' Yehudah and the Sages disagree.

lines.speaker: RL
lines.text: בִּשְׁעַת נְפִילָה נֶחְלְקוּ — they disagree only at the moment the jug is falling. Afterward, all agree he is liable.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: בֵּין בִּשְׁעַת נְפִילָה בֵּין לְאַחַר נְפִילָה הִיא הַמַּחֲלוֹקֶת — the dispute applies in both phases.

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: Objection: if R' Yehudah exempts at the moment of falling, isn't it a קל וחומר that he exempts afterward too?

lines.speaker: RL
lines.text: He is exempt at the moment of falling because אָדָם מַבְקִיר נְזָקָיו בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים — a person abandons his damaged goods. But afterward he has, in effect, re-claimed them.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: A baraita supports both readings. R' Zeira and R' La conclude: even R' Yehudah agrees that מַבְקִיר שׁוֹר שֶׁנָּגַח — one who declares his goring ox hefker — is still liable. לֹא דוֹמֶה נֶזֶק עוֹמֵד לְנֶזֶק מְהַלֵּךְ.

callout.label: Conceptual takeaway:
callout.text: Stationary damage (broken jug shards) can be abandoned. Moving damage (a goring bull) cannot — because while it is in motion, it is still actively expressing its owner's neglect.

slides.title: 🌍 'Joshua's Condition' and the Pouring-Water Mishnah (3:3)
slides.subtitle: The deep reason why some public-domain damage is exempt.
rows: שֶׁעַל מְנָת כֵּן הִנְחִיל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ לְיִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת הָאָרֶץ
Joshua distributed Eretz Yisrael with a built-in clause permitting incidental public-domain use
הָא אִם לֹא הִנְחִיל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ... עַל מְנָת כֵּן חַיָיב
Without that condition, the owner of any abandoned hazard would always be liable
הַשּׁוֹפֵךְ מַיִם בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים וְהוּזַּק בָּהֶן אַחֵר חַיָיב
Pouring water is an active act, not passive abandonment — full liability
Rav Huna: liable for soiled garments; Rav: even soiled garments — exempt
Rav: בּוֹר שֶׁלַּנְּזָקִין פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים — same rule as R' Yochanan

callout.label: Bringing it together:
callout.text: The whole liability scheme of Chapter 3 rests on two layers: (1) Joshua's condition limits the default liability for ordinary public-domain mishaps; (2) within that, the בור-vs-מהלך distinction decides whether vessels are covered. R' Yochanan and Rav agree on the bottom line — pit-style damage spares כלים.

summary.principle: A בור-style hazard pays for personal injury but not for vessels; abandoning damaged property in רה"ר is permitted only because of Joshua's foundational condition — and only as long as the damage has stopped moving.

flowSteps.content:
בּוֹר שֶׁלַּנִּזָּקִין פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים
R' Yochanan's rule
🎓 Bottom line: stationary damage may be abandoned; moving damage (goring ox, poured water) keeps its owner on the hook even after he says הפקר.

quiz.q: What is R' Yochanan's celebrated rule on the daf?
o: בּוֹר שֶׁלַּנִּזָּקִין פָּטוּר עַל הַכֵּלִים — a בור-hazard is exempt for vessel damage
All public-domain damage is exempt
A flask is always treated as personal injury
Wind-blown hazards never generate liability
quiz.e: R' Yochanan applies the Torah's pit-verse (which names שור או חמור) to exempt vessels — פָּטוּר עַל נִיזְקֵי צְלוּחִיתוֹ.

quiz.q: When does R' Eleazar (R' Lazar) say the dispute between R' Yehudah and the Sages applies?
o: Only after the jug has fallen
Only at the moment of falling — afterward all agree he is liable
Only on Shabbat
Only if the jug was already cracked
quiz.e: בִּשְׁעַת נְפִילָה נֶחְלְקוּ אֲבָל לְאַחַר נְפִילָה כָּל עַמָּא מוֹדֵיי שֶׁחַיָיב. R' Yochanan disagrees and applies it to both phases.

quiz.q: What underlying principle does R' Lazar use to explain why R' Yehudah exempts at the moment of falling?
o: The owner cannot be in two places at once
אָדָם מַבְקִיר נְזָקָיו בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים — a person abandons his damaged property in the public domain
The Mishnah is referring only to clay vessels
Public domain is hefker by default
quiz.e: R' Lazar's logic: at the moment of the accident the owner mentally abandons the broken jug. R' Zeira and R' La nuance this for moving hazards (נֶזֶק מְהַלֵּךְ) like a goring bull.

quiz.q: Why does the Yerushalmi invoke 'Joshua's condition'?
o: To exempt women from civil damages
To explain that the right to leave damaged property in רה"ר was built into the original distribution of Eretz Yisrael
To reject the Mishnah
To require a get from the original owner
quiz.e: שֶׁעַל מְנָת כֵּן הִנְחִיל יְהוֹשֻׁעַ לְיִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת הָאָרֶץ — Joshua distributed the land on condition that ordinary public-domain mishaps be tolerated.

quiz.q: According to the principle לֹא דוֹמֶה נֶזֶק עוֹמֵד לְנֶזֶק מְהַלֵּךְ, what is the practical difference?
o: Standing damage pays double; moving damage pays half
Stationary damage can be abandoned (hefker); moving damage (e.g. a goring ox) cannot escape liability through hefker
Standing damage never generates liability
Moving damage requires two witnesses
quiz.e: R' Zeira and R' La: even R' Yehudah agrees that one who declares his goring ox ownerless still pays. While the damage is moving, it still expresses the owner's continued neglect.
